summary: Research the "learn-from-failure" organ (Fig-1 ⚠️ box): which memory systems learn from failure, and what is the honest signal portfolio for a session-based (Claude-Code-host) memory kit
logs: parallel[9] failed: agent({schema}): StructuredOutput retry cap (5) exceeded — 5 failed calls with no valid output

per_system.failure_mechanism: NOT FOUND — store-and-retrieve only. Memory mutation is an LLM-judged CRUD policy driven ONLY by contradiction/dedup/new-info, never by an outcome. In mem0/configs/prompts.py the DEFAULT_UPDATE_MEMORY_PROMPT gives the LLM 4 events: ADD (new info not in memory), UPDATE (info present but "totally different", or same-thing-with-more-detail → keep richer version), DELETE (ONLY "if the retrieved facts contain information that contradicts the information present in the memory" OR explicit user direction to delete), NONE (already present). In mem0/memory/main.py the executors _add_to_vector_store / _update_memory / _delete_memory carry out that LLM decision plus a hash-based dedup on ADD; expired memories are filtered from search, not outcome-pruned. No code path deletes or downweights a memory because it was retrieved and led to a wrong answer / failed task / low utility. GitHub issue #5330 PROPOSES access-frequency decay (access_count * 0.5^(days/7)) — but it is (a) unmerged, proving the feature is absent from core, and (b) recency/frequency-based, still NOT outcome/failure-based.
per_system.outcome_signal: none — the only signal driving a store mutation is the memory-manager LLM's judgment of contradiction / duplication / new-information between the incoming turn's extracted facts and the currently-stored facts. There is no reward, no LLM-judge of answer quality, no re-access reinforcement, no tool-result, no user-correction-of-outcome. Per-memory schema (main.py) stores data/hash/created_at/updated_at/user_id/agent_id/run_id/role/metadata — NO utility/reward/access_count/success/failure field. The only "score" in the codebase is search-time relevance (semantic similarity + BM25 + entity boost); it ranks retrieval and never feeds back into store/update/delete.
per_system.quote: DELETE: "If the retrieved facts contain information that contradicts the information present in the memory, then you have to delete it. Or if the direction is to delete the memory, then you have to delete it." UPDATE: "If the retrieved facts contain information that is already present in the memory but the information is totally different, then you have to update it." (mem0/configs/prompts.py, DEFAULT_UPDATE_MEMORY_PROMPT)
per_system.transferable_to_session_host: Mechanism IS transferable (no benchmark reward needed): mem0's LLM-driven ADD/UPDATE/DELETE/NONE is a frozen-LLM heuristic policy over conversation turns — it runs fine in a bounded, human-in-loop Claude-Code session with no ground-truth oracle, and is a cleaner UPDATE/DELETE than the kit's trust_score dampening. BUT it does NOT close the U-Mem "learn from failure" gap: mem0 is the SAME passive quadrant as the kit — contradiction/dedup-driven curation, zero outcome/failure learning. It would upgrade the kit's curation, not add the missing organ. The failure-learning systems the literature builds ON TOP of mem0 (MemRL, MEM-α, MemQ) all require a scalar task-success reward / QA-accuracy benchmark, which a session host lacks — so THOSE are NOT directly transferable, confirming mem0 itself ships no transferable failure signal because it ships no failure signal at all. Prior claim "nobody ships a usage/outcome signal" is VERIFIED for mem0 core (an access-frequency decay is only an unmerged proposal, and even it is recency- not outcome-based).

per_system.failure_mechanism: NOT FOUND — store-and-retrieve only. Memory maintenance is an LLM sleeptime agent (letta/prompts/system_prompts/sleeptime_v2.py + voice_sleeptime.py) that reorganizes memory blocks via memory tools (create/str_replace/insert/delete/rename in letta/functions/function_sets/base.py, plus rethink_user_memory/rethink). Its edit signal is "contradictory or outdated information" inside the transcript ("Remove or correct outdated or contradictory information"), NOT whether a retrieved memory led to a good/bad OUTCOME. No function downgrades, decays, prunes, or lowers the utility of a memory that produced a wrong answer. Grep across the repo: reinforce=0, decay=0, relevance_score=0, was_helpful=0, usage_count on memory=0. Archival/passage schema (letta/schemas/passage.py) has NO utility/score/access-count/reinforce field — retrieval is pure embedding-similarity + tags. There IS a `feedback: Literal["positive","negative"]` field, but it is on Step (letta/schemas/step.py), set via PATCH /v1/steps/{id}/feedback; step_manager.add_feedback_async merely persists `step.feedback = feedback` + tags and returns — it is NOT read by any memory-edit, retrieval-ranking, or pruning code path. So the outcome signal that exists is decoupled from the memory organ.
per_system.outcome_signal: Human thumbs up/down on a step (Step.feedback = positive|negative, set via REST PATCH /v1/steps/{id}/feedback) — but it is store-and-filter only (listable/filterable), never consumed by memory update or retrieval ranking. The sleeptime memory agent's own edit trigger is contradiction/staleness detected by an LLM reading the transcript, not an outcome/reward.
per_system.transferable_to_session_host: The sleeptime consolidation pattern (background LLM rewrites memory blocks; edit on contradiction/staleness) IS transferable and needs no ground-truth oracle — it's already close to the kit's deferred re-curation. But it does NOT close the learn-from-failure gap: it never asks "did this memory cause a bad outcome," only "is this memory internally stale/contradictory," which the kit's trust_score contradiction path already partially covers. The one genuine outcome signal (Step.feedback thumbs up/down) IS transferable to a session host as a human-in-loop signal and needs no benchmark reward — but in Letta it is inert (nothing reads it back into memory), so there is no wiring to copy, only the raw idea: capture a per-turn human/tool verdict and feed it back to memory utility. That wiring would be net-new for both Letta and the kit.
per_system.quote: feedback: Optional[Literal["positive","negative"]] = Field(None, ...)  # step.py; and add_feedback_async: `step.feedback = feedback` then return — no memory call. Sleeptime prompt: "Update: Remove or correct outdated or contradictory information."

per_system.failure_mechanism: NOT FOUND — store-and-retrieve only. Q1: Graphiti does NOT learn from outcomes/failure. The ONLY update-after-ingest mechanism is contradiction-driven temporal INVALIDATION, not outcome-driven. When a new episode is ingested, `resolve_extracted_edge` (graphiti_core/utils/maintenance/edge_operations.py:~630) calls an LLM prompt (`prompt_library.dedupe_edges.resolve_edge`) that returns a `contradicted_facts` index list; those edges become `invalidation_candidates`, then `resolve_edge_contradictions(resolved_edge, invalidation_candidates)` (line 538) sets `edge.invalid_at = resolved_edge.valid_at` and `edge.expired_at = utc_now()`. That is deterministic temporal bookkeeping — a fact is expired because a NEWER contradicting FACT arrived, never because a retrieved memory led to a bad task result. The `EntityEdge` model (graphiti_core/edges.py:263) has ONLY temporal fields (`valid_at`/`invalid_at`/`expired_at`) — no reward, utility, usage/access_count, hit_count, feedback, outcome, score, decay, boost, or last_used field. A grep for those tokens across search.py (874 lines), search_utils.py (2064 lines), graphiti.py (1793 lines) and edge_operations.py (911 lines) returns ZERO — the only 'usage' hit is `TokenUsageTracker` (LLM billing telemetry), unrelated to memory utility. Q2: The signal driving the one update that exists is LLM-judged semantic/temporal CONTRADICTION between two stored facts (the `resolve_edge` prompt asks the LLM 'Determine which facts the NEW FACT contradicts... the new fact updates/supersedes it' — e.g. job title 'software engineer' -> 'senior engineer'). It is NOT a ground-truth reward, NOT an LLM-judge of outcome quality, NOT a user correction, NOT re-access/re-ask, NOT a tool-result. Retrieval never feeds back into memory strength — there is no store->retrieve->outcome->update loop.
per_system.outcome_signal: none (only signal is LLM-judged fact-vs-fact contradiction, which is a consistency signal, not an outcome/quality signal)
per_system.quote: for edge in invalidation_candidates: ... edge.invalid_at = resolved_edge.valid_at; edge.expired_at = edge.expired_at if edge.expired_at is not None else utc_now(); invalidated_edges.append(edge) — resolve_edge_contradictions(), edge_operations.py:546-571. Prompt: 'Determine which facts the NEW FACT contradicts from either list.'
per_system.transferable_to_session_host: The contradiction-invalidation mechanism IS transferable and needs NO ground-truth oracle — it is exactly the class the kit ALREADY has (trust_score dampen on contradiction/supersession). Graphiti's version is bi-temporal (valid_at/invalid_at with as-of queries) and richer than a scalar dampen, so it's a viable upgrade for the kit's re-curation organ: keep superseded facts with an invalid_at timestamp instead of pruning, enabling 'what did we believe then' queries. BUT it does NOT answer the driving question: graphiti has NO learning-from-failure organ either. It is the SAME passive side as the kit — store + retrieve + contradiction-invalidate. It provides no signal for 'this retrieved memory led to a bad result'. So for U-Mem's missing organ (learn from FAILURE / per-memory utility posteriors), graphiti offers nothing transferable — it confirms, rather than refutes, that this widely-used memory system also ships no usage/outcome signal. Net: transferable as a temporal-invalidation pattern; NOT transferable as a failure-learning mechanism because it has none.

per_system.system: memclaw (caura-ai/caura-memclaw) — governed shared memory for AI agent fleets; pgvector + hybrid search + MCP. Apache-2.0.
per_system.failure_mechanism: TWO code-level mechanisms in core-api/src/core_api/services/evolve_service.py. (1) WEIGHT DAMPEN: _adjust_weights() applies _DELTA_MAP[outcome_type] where constants.py sets EVOLVE_FAILURE_DELTA=-0.15 (vs EVOLVE_SUCCESS_DELTA=+0.1, EVOLVE_PARTIAL_DELTA=+0.03), asymmetric so failures propagate faster, clamped to EVOLVE_WEIGHT_FLOOR=0.05. This weight is a DIRECT retrieval-ranking term: postgres_service.memory_scored_search sets base_score = _similarity_blend*similarity + (1-_similarity_blend)*Memory.weight — so a failed memory ranks lower next recall (loop closes into retrieval, unlike the kit's no-score-hot-path). (2) CORRECTIVE-MEMORY CREATION: on outcome_type in {failure,partial}, _maybe_generate_rule -> _generate_rule sends _RULE_GENERATION_PROMPT to an LLM to synthesize a preventive IF/THEN rule, persisted by _persist_rule as a memory_type='rule' at confidence>=EVOLVE_RULE_CONFIDENCE_THRESHOLD=0.5. This is the autonomous-side (U-Mem Fig 1) 'learn from failure' behavior. SEPARATE auto-inference pipeline (outcome_inference/, the '6 free signals') is mostly aspirational: 3 of 6 extractors return [] as Phase-1 stubs (repeat_recall.py, external_hooks.py explicitly; and cross_agent_reuse is NEUTRAL polarity). The 'repeat-recall = first-answer-failed' signal the prompt named IS a designed failure-signal but is a STUB returning [] pending a Phase-2 memory_recalls table. The 4 live signals (contradictions/supersessions/terminal_memory/cross_agent_reuse) feed forge_service, which ONLY writes skill candidate docs (status='candidate', 'never auto-promote', 'No HITL surfacing') — they do NOT dampen weights or prune. So auto-inferred failure-learning is NOT wired to memory mutation; only the explicit-outcome path is.
per_system.outcome_signal: PRIMARY (shipped, drives the loop): explicit agent-reported outcome via the memclaw_evolve MCP tool / POST /evolve/report — outcome_type in {success,failure,partial} + related_ids (the recall IDs the agent acted on). The acting AGENT self-labels the outcome; there is NO ground-truth reward/oracle. SECONDARY (auto-inferred, mostly stub, feeds only a skill-candidate pipeline not weights): contradiction (contradiction_detector status outdated/conflicted), direct supersession (memories.supersedes_id), terminal-memory keyword classifier (last memory 'shipped/fixed' vs 'blocked/abandoned'), cross-agent-reuse count (recall_count>=3 agents, NEUTRAL), plus two stubs returning [] — repeat_recall (same query re-recalled = first answer failed; needs Phase-2 memory_recalls table) and external_hooks (git/PR/CI status; Phase-5).
per_system.quote: EVOLVE_FAILURE_DELTA = -0.15  # weight decrease on failure (asymmetric — failures propagate faster) || _RULE_GENERATION_PROMPT: "Based on this {outcome_type}, generate a rule that would help avoid this outcome in the future." persisted as memory_type='rule' || base_score = (_similarity_blend * similarity + (1.0 - _similarity_blend) * Memory.weight).label("base_score") || repeat_recall.py: "Phase 1 MVP returns []. ... false positives are worse than false negatives" || forge_service: "Candidates ALWAYS land with status='candidate'. They never auto-promote ... No HITL surfacing — the inbox is Phase 2."
per_system.transferable_to_session_host: PARTIALLY, and the transferable half is the useful half. The PRIMARY signal (agent self-reports outcome_type success/failure/partial + the memory IDs it acted on) needs NO ground-truth oracle and NO benchmark reward — an in-session model can call an evolve-style tool ('I acted on memory X and it was wrong/right'), which fits a bounded, human-in-loop Claude-Code episode cleanly. The two learning ACTIONS both transfer to a markdown/FTS kit: dampen a per-memory weight on failure (kit already has trust_score, just isn't ranked on — memclaw proves ranking on the learned weight is the missing edge, base_score blends it), and LLM-synthesize a corrective 'rule' memory from a failure (a new fact file, well within kit primitives). The AUTOMATED auto-inference signals are the LESS transferable part: contradiction/supersession are already the kit's partial signal; terminal_memory needs a session-status vocabulary; external_hooks/repeat_recall need infra the kit lacks (recall-log table, CI webhooks) — and even in memclaw 3/6 are unshipped stubs and the shipped ones feed only a human-reviewed skill-candidate inbox, not memory mutation. NOTHING here needs a benchmark reward, so all of it is oracle-free-transferable in principle; the honest caveat is that the CHEAP, DEPLOYABLE win is the explicit agent-reported-outcome edge (self-label + weight-dampen + rule-gen), and the 'free passive signals' are mostly a roadmap, not a working autonomous loop, even upstream.

per_system.system: MemOS (MemTensor) — github.com/MemTensor/MemOS, "Self-evolving memory OS for LLM & AI Agents." Paper arXiv 2505.22101 / 2507.03724. Inspected repo code directly (shallow clone, src/memos/*).
per_system.failure_mechanism: NOT FOUND — store-and-retrieve only (as a LIVE mechanism). Three adjacent mechanisms exist, none of which learns from task OUTCOME/failure: (1) mem_feedback/feedback.py MemFeedback — CRUD (add/update/archive via covered_history) driven by EXPLICIT user natural-language feedback (APIFeedbackRequest.content), decided by an LLM-judge (templates/mem_feedback_prompts.FEEDBACK_JUDGEMENT_PROMPT). It is a user-correction handler, not an automatic bad-result detector; nothing observes whether a retrieved memory led to a failure. (2) mem_scheduler/schemas/monitor_schemas.py MemoryMonitorItem.get_importance_score = 0.9*sorting_score(reranker relevance) + 0.05*keywords_score(query-keyword frequency) + 0.05*recording_count — where MemoryMonitorManager.update_memories does `item.recording_count += 1` on RE-RETRIEVAL (memory reappears for a new query), and eviction (memories_to_remove) drops lowest-importance = least-retrieved. This is frequency/recency/relevance, NOT outcome utility. (3) dream/ pipeline — LLM self-reflection/consolidation (reasoning.py CONSOLIDATION_REASONING_PROMPT) that CREATEs InsightMemory; it consumes no outcome signal. The ONE place designed for outcome-utility — DreamMemoryLifecycle.usefulness_score / invalidated_by_feedback / last_hit_at / hit_count (defined in dream/types.py:80-83) — is EXPLICITLY UNIMPLEMENTED: dream/maintenance.py is 'intentionally left unimplemented as a contribution entry point,' and those fields are referenced ONLY in that file's contribution-guide comments (verified: no real code path writes usefulness_score/invalidated_by_feedback anywhere). Retrieval ranking itself is BM25 + graph recall + MemoryReranker (cosine similarity + structural priority; retrieve/reranker.py), never the learned importance_score.
per_system.outcome_signal: No outcome/reward signal. The only content-mutating 'feedback' is EXPLICIT user-supplied natural-language correction (APIFeedbackRequest.content) judged by an LLM into add/update/archive — a human-correction signal, not a task success/failure detector. The scheduler's only usage signal is re-retrieval frequency (recording_count) + query-keyword frequency + reranker relevance — none tied to whether an outcome was good or bad. usefulness_score (the intended low-relevance-feedback signal) is defined but never populated.
per_system.quote: dream/maintenance.py: "This module is responsible for periodic cleanup of Dream-produced memories. It is intentionally left unimplemented as a contribution entry point." + "LOW USEFULNESS — Hit but not helpful ... integrate with the retrieval layer to update usefulness_score when a Dream memory is included in a response but receives low relevance feedback." | monitor_schemas.py: `item.recording_count += 1` (incremented on re-retrieval, not outcome) and importance = `sorting_score*w0 + normalized_keywords_score*w1 + normalized_recording_count_score*w2`, DEFAULT_WEIGHT_VECTOR_FOR_RANKING = [0.9, 0.05, 0.05].
per_system.transferable_to_session_host: The LIVE mechanisms are directly transferable and low-risk for a Claude-Code session host, because none needs a ground-truth oracle or benchmark reward: (a) MemFeedback's explicit user-correction → LLM-judge → add/update/archive maps cleanly onto a human-in-the-loop 'that's wrong, update memory' turn, which the kit already partly has via trust_score dampen-on-contradiction; (b) the scheduler's recording_count/importance is pure re-retrieval-frequency + relevance, computable per-session with no oracle — but note it is exactly the PASSIVE frequency/recency filing-cabinet signal, NOT learning-from-failure, and the kit deliberately does NOT rank the hot path by such a score. The one component that WOULD be learning-from-failure — usefulness_score updated when a retrieved memory 'is included in a response but receives low relevance feedback' — is UNIMPLEMENTED in MemOS, so it offers a design sketch, not code to lift. Even as a sketch it needs a 'was this retrieved memory helpful?' signal, which in a session host with no ground-truth oracle would have to come from a proxy (user correction, an LLM-judge on the turn, or re-ask/contradiction) — precisely the open gap. Net: MemOS confirms the prior claim rather than refuting it — a mainstream, well-resourced memory OS ships explicit-user-feedback + frequency/recency scoring and leaves outcome-usefulness learning as an explicit TODO.

per_system.system: ReasoningBank (arXiv 2509.25140, Google Cloud AI Research / DeepMind; code: github.com/google-research/reasoning-bank, Apache-2.0, Python)
per_system.failure_mechanism: Learns from failure explicitly — it is the paper's headline contribution. In WebArena/prompts/memory_instruction.py there are two DISTINCT distillation prompts: SUCCESSFUL_SI ("extract and summarize useful insights ... based on the agent's successful trajectory") and FAILED_SI, which for a failed trajectory instructs: "You must first reflect and think why the trajectory failed, and then summarize what lessons you have learned or strategies to prevent the failure in the future" and to "Prefer concrete, actionable recovery procedures." Both emit the same schema — ## Title / ## Description / ## Content (paper §3.2: title=strategy identifier, description=one-sentence when/when-NOT-to-use, content=distilled reasoning/guardrails). A PARALLEL_SI variant does self-contrast across mixed success+failure trajectories (MaTTS, §3.3). Failure traces are DISTILLED into preventative "guardrail" memories, not discarded — the exact organ the passive filing cabinet lacks. Retrieval (Appendix A.2): gemini-embedding-001 + cosine top-k (default k=1); NO trust/utility score on the hot path (embedding-similarity only, like the kit's BM25).
per_system.outcome_signal: LLM-as-a-judge, self-supervised, with NO ground-truth. Code: WebArena/prompts/autoeval_prompts.py build_naive_last_frame_eval_prompt/build_naive_multi_frame_eval_prompt send the judge {user intent, final-observation screenshot caption(s), bot's response} and require JSON {"thoughts","status"} with status in {task-possible-bot-success, task-possible-bot-fail, task-impossible}. Paper §3.2: "we adopt an LLM-as-a-judge to label outcomes as success or failure given the query and trajectory, without access to any ground-truth." §3.1: "no ground truth is available during test-time ... leveraging its own past trajectories and any self-verification without relying on external labels." Google blog admits noise but claims robustness: "this self-judgement does not need to be perfectly accurate, as we find ReasoningBank to be quite robust against judgment noise." So the signal is a self-generated correctness verdict, NOT a benchmark reward, user correction, contradiction, or re-access.
per_system.quote: FAILED_SI: "You must first reflect and think why the trajectory failed, and then summarize what lessons you have learned or strategies to prevent the failure in the future." | autoeval: status in {"task-possible-bot-success","task-possible-bot-fail","task-impossible"} | §3.2: "label outcomes as success or failure given the query and trajectory, without access to any ground-truth."
per_system.transferable_to_session_host: Partially — and this is the most transferable of the failure-learning papers because it is explicitly designed to run WITHOUT a benchmark oracle (self-judged, no ground-truth). Two separable pieces: (1) The FAILURE-DISTILLATION prompt pattern is DIRECTLY portable: contrast a success vs failure episode into a titled/described "guardrail" memory needs only a per-episode success/fail label — no reward function. The kit already has the write path; adding a failure-reflection prompt variant is a small, real graft. (2) The SIGNAL is the hard part for a session host. ReasoningBank's judge is domain-specific: it scores web-task success from the FINAL PAGE STATE — a de-facto oracle ("did the page reach the goal?") that a WebArena env conveniently exposes. A Claude-Code session has NO equivalent terminal-state oracle: bounded episodes end when the human walks away, not at a verifiable goal state. The portable analog is an LLM-judge over {user's stated intent for the turn, the turn's tool results/exit codes, whether the user re-asked or corrected in the next turn}, which is weaker and noisier than "final page state" — but the paper's own robustness-to-noise claim (and its no-ground-truth design) is evidence the pattern tolerates a soft signal. Bottom line: the MECHANISM (failure→lesson memory) transfers cleanly; the SIGNAL must be re-sourced from human-in-loop cues (re-ask, correction, tool-error, thumbs) since the session host lacks WebArena's page-state oracle. Not a drop-in, but the closest fit of the failure-learning cohort.

per_system.system: ReMe — "Remember Me, Refine Me: A Dynamic Procedural Memory Framework for Experience-Driven Agent Evolution" (arXiv 2512.10696, Cao et al., ACL 2026 Findings; NUS/Alibaba). Shipped repo: modelscope/ReMe (also mirrored agentscope-ai/ReMe).
per_system.failure_mechanism:
TWO SEPARATE ARTIFACTS — answer differs by which one you mean.

PAPER (the utility-outcome loop DOES exist, code-adjacent detail):
1. UTILITY REFINEMENT / PRUNING (§3.4 Experience Refinement): each memory E tracks retrieval frequency f(E) and historical utility u(E), where "u increments by 1 each time its recall contributes to a successful task completion." Removal rule (Eq. 1): φ_remove(E) = 𝟙[u(E)/f(E) ≤ β] if f(E) ≥ α, else 0, with α=5, β=0.5 — i.e. "an experience is removed when it is frequently retrieved yet fails to improve new task performance." This IS an outcome-driven downward pruning of memories that get retrieved but don't help.
2. FAILURE-AWARE REFLECTION (multi-faceted distillation / self-reflection): on a task FAILURE, LLM_summ analyzes the failed attempt, extracts improvement insights, LLM_execute retries; "when such trial succeeds, the corresponding lessons are incorporated into memory; otherwise, they are discarded" (max 3 reflections). So failures ARE analyzed to seed a corrective retry — but a lesson only survives if the retry SUCCEEDS. Utility is NEVER decremented on failure; a bad memory is only removed via the u/f ratio after ≥5 retrievals. Failed trajectories themselves are transient (not stored). "Selective addition — only trajectories that lead to success are distilled into experiences."

SHIPPED REPO (modelscope/ReMe) — the paper's loop is NOT in the code. The released kit is a store-and-retrieve file-based memory system: steps are auto_memory / auto_resource / dream(extract→integrate) / file_io + hybrid wikilink+BM25+embedding search. Grepped reme/steps/evolve/** — NO u(E)/f(E), no utility field, no α/β pruning threshold, no reflection-retry-on-failure. The `success`/`failed` tokens in integrate.py/extract.py/auto_memory.py are PIPELINE-STEP status (did the integrate step parse/write OK), not task-outcome utility. So the shipped product is passive store-and-retrieve; the utility-outcome refinement is a paper-only research artifact evaluated on benchmarks.
per_system.outcome_signal: Benchmark ground-truth task success (PAPER only). Success = "the agent makes the necessary function calls correctly and yields the expected outputs" (BFCL-V3) / "passes all evaluation tests" (AppWorld). NO LLM-judge for the outcome signal — direct benchmark evaluator. This is the crux for transferability: the utility increment and the reflection-retry loop both consume a ground-truth pass/fail oracle from the benchmark harness.
per_system.quote: φ_remove(E) = 𝟙[u(E)/f(E) ≤ β], if f(E) ≥ α; 0 otherwise  (α=5, β=0.5); "u increments by 1 each time its recall contributes to a successful task completion"; "When such trial succeeds, the corresponding lessons are incorporated into memory; otherwise, they are discarded without cluttering the experience pool."
per_system.transferable_to_session_host:
PARTIALLY, and only after re-sourcing the signal — the paper's mechanism is directly relevant but its outcome signal is NOT available in a Claude-Code session host. Both organs (utility++ and reflection-retry) consume a BENCHMARK GROUND-TRUTH pass/fail oracle (BFCL/AppWorld test evaluator). A session host has bounded episodes, human-in-loop, and NO ground-truth oracle — there is no automated "task passed" signal per turn, and you cannot burn up-to-3 auto-retries of a real user's task. So the mechanism as-published is NOT drop-in.

WHAT IS transferable, and cheaply:
1. The u(E)/f(E) ≤ β pruning rule is the single most portable idea for the kit. It needs a "did this recall help" signal, not a full reward. The kit already tracks retrieval (BM25 hits) = f(E); it just needs a cheap utility proxy. Candidates that fit a session host: (a) user correction/contradiction on a retrieved fact → treat as utility-DOWN (the kit's existing trust_score dampen-on-contradiction is exactly this half-signal — ReMe formalizes it into a retrieve-frequency-normalized prune, which the kit does NOT do since retrieval ranks by BM25 not trust); (b) a memory that is retrieved-into-context but never referenced/acted-on across N sessions → low utility → prune candidate; (c) restatement/re-ask of something already stored → the stored fact failed to surface usefully. None of these need a benchmark.
2. The "only store the lesson if the corrective retry SUCCEEDS" gate is a good anti-pollution rule but its success test needs a proxy in a session host — closest available: the user did not re-raise the problem / did not correct again in the same session (weak, human-in-loop). Honest read: this half is the weaker transfer.
3. KEY GAP the kit shares with ReMe's shipped repo: ReMe's OWN released product (modelscope/ReMe) dropped the utility loop and shipped passive store-and-retrieve — same shape as the kit today. So "the paper has the organ the kit lacks" is true, but "a shipping system implements it" is FALSE for ReMe's code. The transferable insight is the frequency-normalized-utility PRUNE formula (α/β, u/f), fed by the kit's existing contradiction/correction signal instead of a benchmark reward — that turns the kit's partial trust_score dampen into ReMe's remove-when-retrieved-but-unhelpful rule without any ground-truth oracle.

per_system.system: MemRL: Self-Evolving Agents via Runtime Reinforcement Learning on Episodic Memory (arXiv 2601.03192v2)
per_system.failure_mechanism: Runtime RL on episodic memory: each stored memory carries a scalar utility Q, updated after execution by a Monte-Carlo Q-update Q_new ← Q_old + α(r − Q_old) (Eq. 4, a "naturally simplified" TD-learning treating the next state as terminal). r is the environmental reward from executing the retrieved strategy. On a bad outcome r is low, so Q is dampened toward it; on success r is high and Q is reinforced. Failed episodes ARE stored and learned from — the paper explicitly retains ~12% "failure" memories even in high-Q bins as strategically useful near-misses. Retrieval is two-phase: Phase B ranks by a learned-utility-weighted score (Eq. 7) score = (1−λ)·sim̂(Emb(s),Emb(z_i)) + λ·Q̂_i, so the learned utility Q directly ranks/filters what gets retrieved — the exact organ the kit lacks (kit ranks by BM25 only, never by trust_score).
per_system.outcome_signal: Environmental reward r from executing the retrieved action/strategy: "Executing a then yields an environmental reward r (e.g., execution success or scalar score)." On its benchmarks (HLE, BigCodeBench, ALFWorld, Lifelong Agent Bench) r is task-success / execution-pass ("Success Rate = ratio of tasks completed in an epoch") — i.e. a ground-truth benchmark outcome. No LLM-judge and no reward model are used for r (an LLM is used only to summarize the memory text, NOT to assign reward). Q initialization on first write is unspecified in the paper.
per_system.quote: Q_new ← Q_old + α(r − Q_old) [Eq. 4]; "Executing a then yields an environmental reward r (e.g., execution success or scalar score)"; score(s,z_i,e_i) = (1−λ)·sim̂(Emb(s),Emb(z_i)) + λ·Q̂_i [Eq. 7]
per_system.transferable_to_session_host: NOT directly transferable — the whole learning loop is gated on r, an AUTOMATED ENVIRONMENTAL/BENCHMARK reward (execution success, unit tests pass, task-solved label). A Claude-Code session host has bounded episodes, a human in the loop, and NO ground-truth oracle at conversation time — there is no r to plug into the Q-update, so Eq. 4 cannot run as-is. Two things ARE transferable in principle: (a) the retrieval-side idea — rank by a blend of similarity AND a learned utility (Eq. 7's λ·Q̂ term), which is exactly what the kit deliberately does NOT do today (BM25-only, no-score-hot-path-ranking); the kit already computes a trust_score that could play Q's role in a blended score. (b) The Monte-Carlo Q-update SHAPE (nudge utility toward an outcome signal) could be driven by a PROXY reward the session host CAN observe — e.g. the kit's existing partial signals: contradiction/supersession (r≈bad → dampen), restatement/reinforcement (r≈good → reinforce), or a re-access signal. But that is substituting a weak proxy for MemRL's strong ground-truth r; MemRL itself supplies no LLM-judge or oracle-free path. Blunt verdict: MemRL's exact mechanism needs a benchmark reward and is NOT drop-in for a session host; only its architecture (utility-in-the-ranking + MC nudge toward an outcome) transfers, and only if the host manufactures a proxy reward from signals it can actually see.

per_system.system: A-Mem (agiresearch/A-mem, arXiv 2502.12110) — agentic memory: Zettelkasten-style notes with LLM-generated tags/keywords, content-similarity linking, and LLM-driven "memory evolution" of neighbor notes.
per_system.failure_mechanism: NOT FOUND — store-and-retrieve only (plus content-driven note evolution). All mutations (add_note -> analyze_content -> process_memory -> LLM evolution; update; delete) are triggered by embedding similarity + LLM-generated tags, never by a task outcome. No corrective memory on failure, no utility decrement, no pruning of unhelpful memories (delete() is manual-only). Smoking gun in agentic_memory/memory_system.py: MemoryNote declares a retrieval_count field (and last_accessed) but self.retrieval_count = retrieval_count or 0 is set once at init and is NEVER incremented/written anywhere in the file — the usage-utility scaffolding exists but is inert.
per_system.outcome_signal: none — no reward, no LLM-judge of outcome, no user correction, no re-access/re-ask (retrieval_count is dead), no tool-result, no contradiction detection. The only signal is LLM-judged CONTENT relatedness between a new note and its nearest-neighbor notes at WRITE time, which drives linking/evolution — an acquisition/organization signal, not an outcome signal.
per_system.quote: self.retrieval_count = retrieval_count or 0  # set once at init, never incremented anywhere in the file. Evolution prompt asks: "should_evolve": True/False, "actions": ["strengthen","update_neighbor"] — purely on neighbor content relationships.
per_system.transferable_to_session_host: Partially, but NOT for the missing organ. A-Mem's content-evolution (LLM decides whether a new note should strengthen/update neighbor notes) needs no benchmark reward, so it IS transferable to a bounded, no-oracle session host as an organization mechanism. But it is NOT learning-from-failure — it adds nothing to the kit's gap. The transferable takeaway is negative/cautionary: A-Mem is a concrete primary-source precedent that even an "agentic" memory paper ships a retrieval_count utility field and leaves it un-incremented — the same passive filing-cabinet shape as the kit, and actually WEAKER than the kit's trust_score contradiction-dampening. It does not refute "nobody ships an outcome signal"; it confirms it for this system.

synthesis.q1_verdict: REFUTED — but narrowly, and the refutation matters. Tally across 9 systems: 3 YES (memclaw = code; ReasoningBank = code; MemRL = paper), 1 PARTIAL (ReMe = paper-yes / shipped-repo-no), 5 NO (mem0, letta, graphiti, MemOS, A-Mem — all code-verified store-and-retrieve, contradiction/dedup-driven curation only). The prior "nobody ships a usage/outcome signal" claim is FALSE: memclaw (caura-ai/caura-memclaw, Apache-2.0) ships, IN CODE, an explicit agent-reported outcome signal (success/failure/partial via the memclaw_evolve MCP tool) that BOTH dampens a per-memory weight (EVOLVE_FAILURE_DELTA=-0.15, asymmetric) AND blends that weight into retrieval ranking (base_score = sim_blend*similarity + (1-sim_blend)*weight) AND LLM-synthesizes a corrective IF/THEN rule memory — the full autonomous loop, oracle-free. However, the refutation is qualified three ways: (a) the two OTHER code-level yes-systems (ReasoningBank, MemRL) both need an outcome oracle a session host lacks — ReasoningBank a WebArena final-page-state, MemRL a benchmark reward r; (b) ReMe's utility-prune loop exists only in the PAPER — its shipped repo (modelscope/ReMe) dropped it and is passive store-and-retrieve, same shape as the kit; (c) three surveyed systems (A-Mem, MemOS, letta) each ship the SCAFFOLDING for outcome-utility — A-Mem's retrieval_count, MemOS's usefulness_score/hit_count, letta's Step.feedback thumbs — but leave it INERT (never incremented / explicitly-unimplemented TODO / never read back into memory). So the honest headline: exactly ONE surveyed system ships a working, oracle-free, code-level learn-from-failure loop wired into retrieval (memclaw); the kit is NOT below the survey average — with its trust_score contradiction-dampen it is at or slightly above the median (5 of 9 have strictly less), but it sits one clear notch below the one system that closes the loop.
who_learns_from_failure: memclaw (code, MOST convincing — the only oracle-free WORKING loop): explicit agent self-reports outcome_type success/failure/partial + the recall IDs it acted on via the memclaw_evolve MCP tool; _adjust_weights applies an asymmetric delta (failure -0.15 vs success +0.1), the weight is a DIRECT retrieval-ranking term (base_score blends similarity + weight, so a failed memory ranks lower next recall), AND on failure/partial _generate_rule LLM-synthesizes a preventive IF/THEN 'rule' memory. No benchmark reward needed — the acting agent self-labels. This is the exact organ the kit lacks, and it runs in a bounded human-in-loop episode.

ReasoningBank (code, most transferable PATTERN): a dedicated FAILED_SI distillation prompt ('reflect why the trajectory failed, then summarize lessons/strategies to prevent it') turns failure traces into titled 'guardrail' memories, contrasted against SUCCESSFUL_SI. Signal is an LLM-as-judge over the trajectory with NO ground-truth ('label success or failure without access to any ground-truth') — the paper explicitly claims robustness to judge noise. Transferable MECHANISM; but its judge scores web-task FINAL PAGE STATE, a de-facto oracle a session host lacks.

MemRL (paper): each memory carries a scalar utility Q updated by a Monte-Carlo nudge Q_new = Q_old + a(r - Q_old) toward environmental reward r; retrieval ranks by a similarity+Q blend, so utility gates recall. Failed episodes are stored and learned from (~12% retained). But r is an AUTOMATED benchmark/execution reward (tests-pass, task-solved) — no LLM-judge, no oracle-free path; NOT drop-in for a session host.

ReMe (paper only — NOT its shipped repo): a frequency-normalized utility PRUNE (remove when u(E)/f(E) <= 0.5 after f >= 5 retrievals — 'retrieved often yet fails to improve performance') plus failure-reflection-retry (a lesson survives only if the corrective retry succeeds). Consumes a benchmark ground-truth pass/fail. Its OWN shipped code (modelscope/ReMe) dropped this loop entirely and is passive store-and-retrieve — so 'a shipping system implements it' is FALSE for ReMe.

signal_portfolio.signal: Tool-result / exit-code after acting on a recalled fact (build passed, test green, command 0 vs non-zero)
signal_portfolio.source_in_session: the Bash/tool output in the same turn the model applied a recalled memory
signal_portfolio.gameable_or_noisy: attribution is the hard part — a green build doesn't prove THIS fact caused it, and a red build may fail for reasons unrelated to the recalled memory; needs the model to name which memory it acted on (memclaw's related_ids pattern) or the link is guesswork
signal_portfolio.precedent: memclaw (explicit related_ids + external_hooks CI signal, though external_hooks is a Phase-5 stub); MemRL's r is execution-success in the same family but oracle-backed

signal_portfolio.signal: User correction / contradiction immediately after a recall (the retrieved fact was wrong)
signal_portfolio.source_in_session: the next user turn contradicting or fixing a fact the model just surfaced
signal_portfolio.gameable_or_noisy: failure-biased by construction — only fires when something was wrong, never confirms a good recall, so used alone it can only prune, never reinforce; also conflates 'fact was stale' with 'model mis-applied a correct fact'
signal_portfolio.precedent: letta Step.feedback (negative) — but inert; MemOS MemFeedback (explicit user NL correction → LLM-judge → add/update/archive); the kit's own trust_score dampen-on-contradiction is this half-signal already

signal_portfolio.signal: cmk forget / user edits or deletes a memory file
signal_portfolio.source_in_session: an explicit cmk forget call or a hand-edit to context/memory
signal_portfolio.gameable_or_noisy: cleanest and most explicit signal, zero inference — but rare (users seldom bother), failure-only (removal, never reinforcement), and says nothing about the many facts the user never touches
signal_portfolio.precedent: mem0 DELETE (explicit user direction to delete); letta memory delete tool; novel as a LEARNING signal — no surveyed system feeds an explicit forget back into a utility posterior

signal_portfolio.signal: Recalled fact was injected but then re-searched / re-asked (recall-miss: the snapshot didn't surface it usefully, so the model or user went looking again)
signal_portfolio.source_in_session: a cmk search / re-ask in a session where that fact was already in the injected snapshot
signal_portfolio.gameable_or_noisy: strong IN THEORY as a miss-signal but needs a recall-log table the kit lacks; heavy confound — a re-search may be for a DIFFERENT facet, not because the injected fact failed; memclaw's own repeat_recall extractor is a Phase-1 STUB returning [] precisely because 'false positives are worse than false negatives' here
signal_portfolio.precedent: memclaw repeat_recall (designed as 'same query re-recalled = first answer failed' — but UNSHIPPED stub); MemOS recording_count (re-retrieval frequency, but used as importance-UP not miss-signal); ReMe f(E) frequency term

signal_portfolio.signal: Was the recalled fact actually USED/referenced in the model's answer (vs injected-but-ignored)
signal_portfolio.source_in_session: cross-referencing the injected snapshot against the model's produced turn
signal_portfolio.gameable_or_noisy: the single most informative passive signal AND the hardest to detect reliably — 'used' is a fuzzy judgment (paraphrase, implicit influence), so it needs an LLM-judge over (snapshot, answer) which adds cost and its own noise; injected-but-ignored may just mean the fact was irrelevant to THIS turn, not low-utility
signal_portfolio.precedent: MemOS usefulness_score is designed for exactly this ('hit but not helpful') but is EXPLICITLY UNIMPLEMENTED; A-Mem retrieval_count exists but is never incremented (inert) — nobody ships it working

signal_portfolio.signal: Task outcome under an explicit /goal or acceptance-criteria block (pass/fail against user-declared done-criteria)
signal_portfolio.source_in_session: a user-declared goal/done-criteria at turn start + its verifiable resolution
signal_portfolio.gameable_or_noisy: the closest a session host gets to a real oracle — but only exists when the USER declares a checkable goal (most turns don't), and 'pass' is often self-judged by the model, reintroducing the LLM-judge noise ReasoningBank tolerates but doesn't eliminate
signal_portfolio.precedent: ReasoningBank (LLM-judge success/fail over trajectory, no ground-truth); MemRL/ReMe (benchmark pass/fail — but those ARE oracles the session host lacks)

signal_portfolio.source_in_session: a newer fact conflicting with an older one at write time
signal_portfolio.gameable_or_noisy: already shipped in the kit (trust_score dampen) and in 5 surveyed systems — but it is a CONSISTENCY signal, not an OUTCOME signal: it tells you two facts disagree, never whether either one led to a good/bad result; over-firing on benign restatements is a known noise mode
signal_portfolio.precedent: graphiti (temporal invalidation), mem0 (DELETE-on-contradiction), letta (sleeptime staleness), MemOS — the universal baseline; the kit already has it

signal_portfolio.signal: User re-states the SAME rule they already gave (recurrence of an assertion)
signal_portfolio.source_in_session: a repeated user directive that already exists as a fact (the kit's write-fact.mjs bumpRecurrence site)
signal_portfolio.gameable_or_noisy: the Task-181 trap: recurrence measures FRICTION not success — a rule the model follows perfectly is never re-stated, so it stays at recurrence_count 1 while a rule the model KEEPS BREAKING climbs; read as 'importance/adherence' it is INVERTED (the best rule scores lowest). Only honestly usable as a FAILURE/friction signal ('you keep having to remind me → surface this, the rule is failing'), never as a promote-on-strength signal
signal_portfolio.precedent: kit's own recurrence gate (Task 151/181); memclaw repeat_recall shares the identical 'repeat = first-answer-failed' friction insight; ReMe f(E) as a denominator (frequency alone is not utility)

synthesis.kit_gap_named: The kit has the acquire→retrieve→consistency-curate loop (auto-extract → BM25 inject → trust_score dampen on contradiction/supersession) but is missing the U-Mem Figure-1 ⚠️-box organ entirely: nothing ever asks "did this RETRIEVED memory lead to a good or bad OUTCOME" and feeds that verdict back — there is no outcome/utility posterior per memory, no failure-distilled corrective memory, and (compounding it) even the consistency signal it does compute never reaches retrieval ranking, which is deliberately BM25-only (no-score-hot-path).
synthesis.transferable_recommendation: Two mechanisms, both oracle-free and both proven in memclaw's CODE (not just a paper): (1) an EXPLICIT agent-reported-outcome edge — the session model, when it acts on a recalled fact, self-labels the result (memclaw's memclaw_evolve: outcome_type + the recall IDs acted on), which (a) dampens that fact's trust_score on failure/partial and (b) triggers an LLM-synthesized corrective 'rule' fact-file from a failure (both are within existing kit primitives — trust_score already exists; a rule is just a fact write); and (2) rank retrieval by a similarity+trust BLEND instead of pure BM25 (memclaw's base_score, MemRL's Eq.7 λ·Q̂) so the learned signal actually changes what surfaces — the kit deliberately does NOT do this today, and it is the single edit that turns the already-computed trust_score from decorative into load-bearing. HONEST CAVEAT: a session host has NO ground-truth oracle — ReasoningBank's page-state, MemRL's benchmark r, and ReMe's test-pass are all unavailable when a bounded, human-in-loop episode ends because the user walked away, not at a verifiable goal. So the outcome signal MUST be a PORTFOLIO of weak proxies (tool-result, user-correction, cmk-forget, /goal, recall-miss), each individually noisy or failure-biased, aggregated for direction — and it must NEVER read the recurrence/re-statement signal as success (Task-181: recurrence is friction; the best-followed rule scores lowest). What breaks without an oracle: you cannot reinforce a GOOD silent recall (nothing observes it), so an oracle-free kit can prune-on-failure far more reliably than it can promote-on-success — the honest asymmetry to design around, and the reason no surveyed system offers a clean, drop-in session-host answer.
synthesis.adr_thesis_line: A Claude-Code session is a bounded, human-in-loop episode with no ground-truth oracle, so the kit closes U-Mem's missing learn-from-failure organ not with one reward signal but with a PORTFOLIO of weak, mostly failure-biased proxies (tool-result, user-correction, cmk-forget, /goal, recall-miss — never recurrence, which is friction not success) aggregated to dampen a per-memory utility that finally ranks retrieval, borrowing memclaw's oracle-free self-reported-outcome + weight-in-ranking + corrective-rule loop while accepting that without an oracle the kit can prune-on-failure far more reliably than it can reinforce-on-success.
synthesis.honesty_note: Evidence grading: 6 of 9 findings are CODE-level (mem0, letta, graphiti, memclaw, MemOS, A-Mem — grep-verified schemas/prompts/functions), which is the strong tier. 3 are weaker: ReasoningBank is code but the SIGNAL claim rests partly on paper §3.1/§3.2 quotes; MemRL and MemRL's transferability are PAPER-ONLY (arXiv 2601.03192v2 — no repo inspected, so Q-init and exact wiring are UNVERIFIED beyond the equations quoted); ReMe is the trickiest — its learn-from-failure loop is PAPER-only and its own SHIPPED repo (modelscope/ReMe) was grepped and confirmed to LACK it, so any claim that 'ReMe implements' the prune is false at the code level. The prior 'nobody ships a usage/outcome signal' claim is REFUTED specifically by memclaw's CODE — but memclaw itself is early: 3 of its 6 'free passive signals' are Phase-1 STUBS returning [] (repeat_recall, external_hooks) or NEUTRAL (cross_agent_reuse), and its auto-inferred signals feed only a human-reviewed skill-candidate inbox, NOT weight mutation — only the EXPLICIT agent-reported path is wired to weights+rules, so 'memclaw ships the full autonomous loop' is true only for the explicit-outcome edge, not the passive-signal roadmap. UNVERIFIED and NOT independently checked by me in this synthesis: the kit's own current trust_score internals and the BM25-only ranking claim are taken from the prompt's framing + the kit's own memory file (project_signal-portfolio-...md, trust: medium, recurrence 1) and DECISION-LOG D-246/Task-181, not re-derived from packages/cli source this pass. Do not overstate: no surveyed system offers a clean, drop-in, oracle-free session-host learn-from-failure loop — memclaw is the closest and it is early-stage and partly stubbed.

workflowProgress.promptPreview:
CONTEXT — you are researching for "claude-memory-kit", a per-project in-repo memory system for
